title: World: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 26 May 2026 (pdf)
summary: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 26 May 2026 Legend Cases reported are increasing or peaking Cases reported are decreasing or circulation is ongoing. Since 1st January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,029 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,446 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking.
bullets: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 26 May 2026 Legend Cases reported are increasing or peaking Cases reported are decreasing or circulation is ongoing.

Since 1st January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,029 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,446 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking.

The Ministry of Health officially declared the country dengue-free on 25 May 2026 after more than two incubation periods (26 days) without any reported cases, meeting the criteria for declaring the end of the dengue outbreak.
This follows the scale-down of Operation Namu26 on 24 April 2026, with routine surveillance and vector control measures continuing to sustain the dengue-free status.
– Source: Cook Islands Dengue Free - Te Marae Ora Cook Islands Ministry of Health accessed on 26 May 2026.

full_text:
Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 26 May 2026
Legend Cases reported are increasing or peaking
Cases reported are decreasing or circulation is ongoing.
Awaiting confirmation of aetiology.
No update for more than 2 weeks
DENV – Dengue Virus
For the Pacific Public 
Health Surveillance 
Network (PPHSN)
NEW ZEALAND
HAWAII
An interactive version of the map is available online at: 
www.spc.int/phd/epidemics/ 
POLIOVIRUS
PERTUSSIS
PERTUSSIS
DENV-1 & DENV-2
MEASLES
DENV-2
DENV-1 & DENV-2
DENV-1 & DENV-2
DENV-1
ROTAVIRUS
PERTUSSIS (Pohnpei)
PERTUSSIS
DENV-2
Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 19 May 2026:
Dengue
• Cook Islands: Dengue transmission in the Cook Islands has been interrupted, with no new cases reported since 28 
April 2026. The Ministry of Health officially declared the country dengue-free on 25 May 2026 after more than two 
incubation periods (26 days) without any reported cases, meeting the criteria for declaring the end of the dengue 
outbreak. This follows the scale-down of Operation Namu26 on 24 April 2026, with routine surveillance and vector 
control measures continuing to sustain the dengue-free status. The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 is removed. 
– Source: Cook Islands Dengue Free - Te Marae Ora Cook Islands Ministry of Health accessed on 26 May 2026.
• Kiribati: Dengue transmission continues in Kiribati, with the outbreak ongoing since April 2025. As of 26 May 2026, 
a cumulative total of 535 laboratory-confirmed cases and 1,583 suspected cases have been reported nationwide, 
including 20 new confirmed cases and 19 new suspected cases since 13 April 2026. Transmission remains 
concentrated in South Tarawa, with both DENV-1 and DENV-2 circulating, and most cases reported as mild, 
although one death and 68 hospitalisations have been recorded to date. The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 
remains in effect. – Source: Dengue Fever Situational Report 33 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point 
on 26 May 2026.
• New Caledonia: As of 23 May 2026, dengue transmission remains ongoing in New Caledonia, with 1,837 cases 
reported in 2026. The outbreak is driven by locally acquired infections, with DENV-1 as the predominant serotype. 
Environmental conditions favourable to mosquito breeding, together with population movement, continue to 
support transmission, and health authorities are reinforcing early care-seeking and vector control measures. The
red alert for DENV-1 remains in effect. – Source: La dengue | Direction des Affaires Sanitaires et Sociales de
Nouvelle-Calédonie accessed on 26 May 2026.
• Samoa: As of EpiWeek 20 (11–17 May 2026), dengue transmission continues in Samoa, with 141 new cases 
reported, representing a 7% increase compared with the previous week, and eight new hospital admissions with no 
ICU cases or deaths. DENV-1 (54%) and DENV-2 (46%) continue to co-circulate, with transmission widespread across 
Upolu (64%) and Savai‘i (36%), and children under 15 years accounting for the majority of cases (74%). Since 1st 
January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,029 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,446 laboratory-confirmed cases have 
been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on 
community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking. The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 remains 
in effect. – Source: Dengue-sitrep-issue-no-57 accessed on 26 May 2026.
• Tonga: As of 25 May 2026, dengue transmission continues in Tonga, with a total of 66 laboratory-confirmed cases 
reported, including two new cases since the 22 May 2026. Two cases are currently hospitalised, with no dengue-
related deaths reported to date. The epidemiological profile remains unchanged, with the highest burden among 
children aged 11–14 years, predominantly male, and most cases reported from Tongatapu. DENV-2 remains the 
identified circulating serotype, and response activities, including community awareness campaigns and vector 
control measures, are ongoing to limit further transmission. The red alert for DENV-2 remains in effect. – Source: 
Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report #23 - Ministry of Health Tonga - Official Facebook page accessed on 26 
May 2026.
Pertussis/Whooping cough
• New Zealand: Pertussis activity in New Zealand continues as part of the ongoing national epidemic, with 
transmission persisting nationwide. As of the week ending 15 May 2026, 48 cases were reported, an increase 
compared with 44 cases in the previous week. This brings the cumulative total for 2026 to 734 cases. The blue alert 
for pertussis remains in effect. – Source: Pertussis dashboard accessed on 26 May 2026.
Other Information:
Dengue
• Federated States of Micronesia, Kosrae State: Dengue activity in Kosrae State, Federated States of Micronesia, 
remains under monitoring, with three cases reported over the past two weeks, including two newly confirmed and 
unlinked cases detected through laboratory surveillance. The affected individuals have been managed clinically, 
with cases reported as isolated, treated and discharged, and no evidence of widespread transmission identified to 
date. Given the presence of the mosquito vector on the island, public health authorities are maintaining 
surveillance and implementing vector control and community clean-up measures to reduce the risk of further 
transmission. – Source: FSM weekly surveillance report (May 4-14, 2026 – EpiWeek 19 & 20) shared with PPHSN 
Coordinating Body Focal point on 22 May 2026.
• New Zealand: In New Zealand, during EpiWeek 20 (16 – 22 May 2026), two confirmed imported dengue cases were 
reported. The confirmed cases had each been to the Cook Islands and Samoa during the incubation periods. Given 
the known serological cross-reactivity between dengue and other flaviviruses, some reported dengue cases may 
ultimately be reclassified as other flavivirus infections as epidemiological and laboratory investigations continue. – 
Source: NZ Arbovirus Notifications by Country (20: 16/05/2026–22/05/2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body 
Focal point on 25 May 2026.
• Vanuatu: From EpiWeek 01 to EpiWeek 19, 2026, a total of eight dengue cases have been reported in Vanuatu, all 
on Efate Island, with no hospitalisations or deaths recorded. Cases are primarily concentrated in the Pango Area 
Council (88%), with the remaining cases reported in Port Vila, and epidemiological investigations have identified 
links between several cases, including recent travel to Fiji within the incubation period. Although case numbers 
remain below the alert threshold and no outbreak has been declared, a slight increase in cases has been observed 
and health authorities are continuing close surveillance to monitor potential changes in transmission. – Source: 
Vanuatu Dengue Situation Update 01 (18 May 2026) on PacNet on 26 May 2026.
Diphtheria
• Australia: Diphtheria activity in Australia has increased markedly since late 2025, with 221 confirmed cases 
reported in 2026 as of 18 May, representing a substantial rise compared with previous years. The outbreak is 
concentrated in the Northern Territory (60.2%) and Western Australia (35.7%), with most cases locally acquired and 
occurring in remote and very remote areas, particularly among Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations. 
Both cutaneous (67.9%) and respiratory (31.7%) presentations are reported, with no confirmed diphtheria-related 
deaths reported to date in 2026, although one death remains under investigation, and public health response 
measures including enhanced surveillance, vaccination, contact tracing and infection control are ongoing. – Sources: 
Diphtheria outbreak update, Diphtheria declared a Communicable Disease Incident of National Significance and 
Diphtheria in Australia – Epidemiological update – 18 May 2026 accessed on 26 May 2026.
Ebola virus
• Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda: As of 21–22 May 2026, the outbreak of Ebola disease caused by 
Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Uganda remains ongoing and has been 
designated a Public Health Emergency of International Concern by WHO on May 16, 2026, with 85 confirmed cases 
and 10 confirmed deaths reported across both countries, including two imported confirmed cases and one death in 
Uganda. In DRC, 83 confirmed cases and 746 suspected cases, including 176 suspected deaths, have been reported 
across Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu provinces, with transmission concentrated in Mongbwalu, Rwampara and 
Bunia. High population mobility with humanitarian challenges, weak contact follow-up and inadequate isolation and 
referral systems continue to complicate response efforts. With no licensed vaccines or specific therapeutics 
currently available for Bundibugyo virus disease, response measures continue to focus on surveillance, contact 
tracing, infection prevention and control, case management, safe and dignified burials, and cross-border 
coordination, while WHO temporary recommendations do not currently advise travel or border closures. – Sources: 
Epidemic of Ebola Disease caused by Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda 
determined a public health emergency of international concern, Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus, 
Democratic Republic of the Congo & Uganda and Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus – Democratic Republic 
of the Congo accessed on 26 May 2026.
Murray Valley Encephalitis
• Australia, Northern Territory: Recent detections of Murray Valley encephalitis (MVE) virus have been reported in 
the Northern Territory, including two confirmed cases in Alice Springs, both of whom died, and virus detection in 
mosquitoes in the Greater Darwin region. Increased rainfall during the wet season has contributed to higher 
mosquito populations, elevating the risk of transmission of this rare but potentially severe mosquito-borne 
infection affecting the central nervous system. Enhanced mosquito surveillance and control activities are ongoing, 
and clinicians are advised to consider MVE in patients presenting with compatible symptoms, particularly during the 
seasonal risk period from February to July. – Source: Murray Valley encephalitis | NT Health accessed on 26 May 
2026.

related.excerpt: THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 ICRC 0173/002 05.2010 10,000 Mission The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) is an impartial, neutral and independent organization whose exclusively humanitarian mission is to protect the lives and dignity of victims of armed conflict and other situations of violence and to provide them with assistance. The ICRC also endeavours to prevent suffering by promoting and strengthening humanitarian law and universal humanitarian principles. Established in 1863, the ICRC is at the origin of the Geneva Conventions and the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement. It directs and coordinates the international activities conducted by the Movement in armed conflicts and other situations of violence. related.title: United Nations Charter
related.summary: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.
bullets: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.

Article 5 / A Member of the United Nations against which preventive or enforcement action has been taken by the Security Council may be suspended from the exercise of the rights and privileges of mem- bership by the General Assembly upon the recom- mendation of the Security Council.

Article 6 A Member of the United Nations which has persistently violated the Principles contained in the present Charter may be expelled from the Organization by the General Assembly upon the recommendation of the Security Council.

Accordingly, our respective Governments, through representatives assembled in the city of San Francisco, who have exhibited their full powers found to be in good and due form, have agreed to the present Charter of the United Nations and do hereby establish an international organization to be known as the United Nations.
CHAPTER I PURPOSES AND PRINCIPLES Article 1 The^Purposes of the United Nations are: 1.
related.excerpt: CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS AND STATUTE OF THE INTERNATIONAL COURT OF JUSTICE SAN FRANCISCO • 1945 CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS WE THE PEOPLES OF THE UNITED NATIONS DETERMINED to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war, which twice in our life- time has brought untold sorrow to mankind, and to reaffirm faith in fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person, in the equal rights of men and women and of nations large and small, and to establish conditions under which justice and respect for the obligations arising from treaties and other sources of international law can be maintained, and to promote social progress and better standards of life in larger freedom, AND FOR THESE ENDS to practice tolerance and live together in peace with one another as good neighbors, and to unite our strength to maintain international peace and security, and to ensure, by the acceptance of principles and the institution of methods, that armed force shall not be used, save in the common interest, and to employ international machinery for the promotion of the economic and social advancement of all peoples, HAVE RESOLVED TO COMBINE OUR EFFORTS TO ACCOMPLISH THESE AIMS

related.title: UN Peacekeeping Capstone Doctrine
related.summary: 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking. During this time, the conduct of United Nations peacekeeping operations has been guided by a largely unwritten body of principles and informed by the experiences of the many thousands of men and women who have served in the more than 60 operations launched since 1948. United Nations Peacekeeping Operations Principles and Guidelines 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking.

related.title: Consolidated Sanctions List
related.summary: Initially convicted on 23 May 2014 by the ICC to 12 years in prison for war crimes and crimes against humanity, the ICC Appeals Chamber reduced his sentence and determined that Katanga’s sentence should be completed on 18 January 2016. Arrested by Congolese authorities in October 2005, acquitted by the Court of Appeal in Kisangani, subsequently transferred to the judicial authorities in Kinshasa on new charges of crimes against humanity, war crimes, murder, aggravated assault and battery.

As of June 2011, resides in Kinshasa. Since 2010, Kakolele has been involved in activities apparently on behalf of the DRC government’s Programme de Stabilisation et Reconstruction des Zones Sortant des Conflits Armés (STAREC), including participation in a STAREC mission to Goma and Beni in March 2011. DRC authorities arrested him in December 2013 in Beni, North Kivu Province, for allegedly blocking the DDR process. He left the DRC and lived in Kenya for some time, before being called back by the DRC Government to assist them with the situation in the Territory of Beni. related.title: Britain ‘sleepwalking into a food crisis’ without urgent action, experts say
related.summary: They highlighted three priorities on which they said ministers should concentrate: resilient domestic production of healthier food; greater preparedness for supply chain shocks; and access for all to safe, affordable and healthy food. Britain is “sleepwalking into a food crisis” caused by extreme weather, inflation and the impacts of the Iran war – and the government is failing to take the threat seriously, food experts have said.
bullets: They highlighted three priorities on which they said ministers should concentrate: resilient domestic production of healthier food; greater preparedness for supply chain shocks; and access for all to safe, affordable and healthy food.
Britain is “sleepwalking into a food crisis” caused by extreme weather, inflation and the impacts of the Iran war – and the government is failing to take the threat seriously, food experts have said.
Even if the Iran war is resolved soon, fuel and fertiliser prices will stay high until the supply crunch through the strait of Hormuz can be eased.

Tim Lang, a professor emeritus of food policy at City St George’s, University of London, said the government’s current strategy amounted to little more than “business as usual” and that warnings were not being heeded.

“Whether we see food security as an issue of escalating food poverty and deepening cost of living squeeze or as the ‘hard’ version of security as defence, there are no grounds for complacency.” Ministers have failed to make the connections and are behind the public in awareness and readiness to act, according to Lang.
related.excerpt: Britain is “sleepwalking into a food crisis” caused by extreme weather, inflation and the impacts of the Iran war – and the government is failing to take the threat seriously, food experts have said. Farmers are facing severe strain from the current heatwave following a dry spring, with many crops likely to yield less as temperatures rise beyond their tolerance. Livestock are also suffering heat stress and there is a rising risk of wildfires. Economic losses are likely to be measured in the hundreds of millions of pounds. Food prices were already on track to be 50% higher this November than they were five years ago, and the current weather – with more heatwaves likely to follow in the summer, when temperatures could top 40C – is adding to the inflationary pressure. Even if the Iran war is resolved soon, fuel and fertiliser prices will stay high until the supply crunch through the strait of Hormuz can be eased. Last week, Rachel Reeves, the chancellor, floated the idea of voluntary price caps on staple foods, but was knocked back by supermarkets and opposition parties. A group of food experts have written to ministers this week calling for the national food strategy to be updated to
